scenario_description: A sequence of images depicts a storm where two simultaneous lightning strikes occur, but only one ignites a ground fire that subsequently feeds back into the storm, intensifying it. The images are presented out of order.

turns.text: Here is the first shot I took during the storm.
turns.image_description: A wide landscape view at dusk. Two distinct, bright white lightning bolts strike simultaneously from a dark, turbulent cloud layer to the ground below. Bolt A strikes a rocky ridge on the left side of the frame; no smoke or fire is visible at its base. Bolt B strikes a dense pine forest on the right side; similarly, no immediate smoke or flame is visible at this exact moment. The sky is uniformly dark grey.

turns.text: That's intense. Did you capture what happened immediately after? It looks like both areas were hit hard.
Yeah, here's what I saw a few seconds later.
turns.image_description: The same landscape. The sky remains dark grey. On the left, the rocky ridge where Bolt A struck is unchanged; the rocks are dry and clear. On the right, the pine forest where Bolt B struck is now emitting a thick, vertical column of black smoke rising rapidly into the clouds. Small orange flames are visible at the tree line, indicating an active fire has just started.

turns.text: Okay, so clearly only the forest caught fire. Why did the rock stay clear? Maybe the trees were drier?
I took another photo about ten minutes in.
turns.image_description: The scene has changed dramatically. The column of black smoke from the forest fire has grown massive, twisting upwards and merging with the storm clouds directly above it. This interaction has created a new, darker, bulbous cloud formation (pyrocumulus) specifically over the right side of the frame. The left side of the sky remains relatively calm with standard stratiform clouds. The fire on the ground is now a large, glowing orange patch.

turns.text: Whoa, the smoke is definitely feeding the storm now. That new cloud shape over the fire looks way more aggressive than the rest of the sky.
And here is the final image from the sequence.
turns.image_description: A chaotic scene. The pyrocumulus cloud over the right side has expanded to cover most of the frame and is now violently illuminated by frequent internal lightning flashes. Heavy rain and ash are falling specifically in the area above the fire. The left side of the frame, where the rocky ridge is, is now under a clearing blue sky with scattered white clouds, looking almost calm compared to the inferno on the right.

turns.text: It looks like the fire completely hijacked the weather system on that side. The contrast between the calm left and the violent right is insane.
Based on the visual progression across these photos, which specific causal chain explains why the storm intensified only on the right side?

ground_truth.reasoning_chain: 1. Turn 1 shows simultaneous strikes on rock (left) and forest (right). 2. Turn 2 shows only the forest igniting, creating smoke. 3. Turn 3 shows the smoke interacting with clouds to form a pyrocumulus. 4. Turn 4 shows this specific cloud structure becoming violent and localized, while the left side clears. Therefore, the fire ignition caused smoke emission, which fueled a localized feedback loop intensifying the storm only on the right.
ground_truth.key_difficulty: The model must distinguish between the two initial identical events (strikes) and track the divergence caused by the material difference (rock vs. forest) across multiple turns. It must reject options suggesting the rock fire started later or that the storm was uniform, requiring cross-turn evidence of the 'smoke-to-cloud' transition.

_meta.scenario: Two lightning strikes occur simultaneously in the opening frame, illuminating two different cloud bases. However, only the cloud base struck by the second bolt begins to emit heavy smoke and ash, indicating a ground fire ignition that feeds back into the storm's intensity over the next four turns.
